metric.title: Metric Playground
metric.subtitle: Drag the <queryPoint>query point</queryPoint> and switch metrics to see how the nearest neighbors change.
metric.l2Desc: Measures the straight-line distance between two points. The concentric circles show equidistant zones — all points on the same circle are equally "far" from the query.
metric.cosineDesc: Measures the angle between two vectors from the origin. Distance from origin does not matter — only direction. The arcs show the angular difference between query and each neighbor.
metric.ipDesc: Measures how much a vector aligns with the query direction, weighted by magnitude. The dashed perpendicular lines show equal-projection zones. Points further along the query direction score higher.
metric.explainerP1: A metric is just a function that takes two vectors and returns a single number telling you how similar (or different) they are. Vector search is, at its core, "find the K vectors with the best metric score against my query." Everything else — indexes, clusters, graphs — is just bookkeeping to avoid computing this score against every vector in the database.
metric.explainerP2: The metric you choose defines what "similar" means for your data. Two embeddings can be "near" under one metric and "far" under another — same data, different answer. That's why this choice happens before you ever build an index.
metric.threeInPractice: The three you'll see in practice
metric.l2Explain: L2 (Euclidean): straight-line distance. Lower is better. Use when both direction and magnitude matter — image embeddings, geographic coordinates, raw sensor readings. The default for most CV models.
metric.cosineExplain: Cosine: angle between vectors, ignoring length. Higher is better. Use when only direction matters — text embeddings (BERT, OpenAI, etc.), recommendation systems where vector "popularity" shouldn't dominate.
metric.ipExplain: Inner Product (IP): dot product, rewards both alignment AND magnitude. Higher is better. Use with models that explicitly normalize for IP (DPR, many retrieval models). Mathematically identical to Cosine when vectors are unit-length.
metric.matchMetric: Match the metric to the model. Embedding models are trained with a specific metric in mind. Using a different one at search time will silently wreck your recall. Check the model card before picking.
metric.stayConsistent: Stay consistent. All vectors in a collection must use the same metric. You cannot mix them within a single index, and you cannot meaningfully compare scores across different metrics.
metric.tryThis: Try this: drag the query point near the origin and switch between Cosine and L2. Watch how Cosine cares only about which "direction" the query is pointing — points on the opposite side of the origin can be "close" under L2 but "far" under Cosine. That visual is the entire concept.

ivf.title: FLAT vs IVF
ivf.subtitle: Same data, same query — but a different search strategy. Drag the query point, adjust nprobe, and press Run Search to see how IVF skips most of the data.
ivf.step1: Build phase (already done): K-means partitioned the {{points}} points into {{k}} clusters. Each cluster has a centroid (the × marks).
ivf.step2: Step 1 — evaluate centroids: IVF first computes the distance from the query to every centroid. That's only {{k}} comparisons, regardless of dataset size.
ivf.step3: Step 2 — pick the closest nprobe clusters: these light up. All other clusters are skipped entirely.
ivf.step4: Step 3 — scan points inside the chosen clusters: only ~{{scanned}} of the {{total}} points.
ivf.takeaway: The tradeoff: larger nprobe → more clusters scanned → higher recall but slower. Smaller nprobe → faster but you may miss true neighbors that live in unscanned clusters. Try setting nprobe = 1 and dragging the query near a cluster boundary to see recall drop.

hnsw.title: HNSW Explorer
hnsw.subtitle: Watch a search travel through the layered graph — from the sparse top layer (highways) down to the dense bottom layer (local roads). Drag the query point on Layer 0 and tune M / ef to see how the graph changes.
hnsw.stepEnter: Enter at the top layer (Layer {{layer}}) at node {{node}}.
hnsw.stepEvaluate: At Layer {{layer}}: evaluate {{count}} neighbor(s) of node {{node}}.
hnsw.stepMove: Layer {{layer}}: move from node {{from}} → node {{to}} (closer to query).
hnsw.step1: Layered graph: each node is randomly assigned a max layer using an exponential distribution. Most nodes only live at Layer 0; very few reach the top.
hnsw.step2: Within each layer: every node connects to its M nearest neighbors. Higher layers are sparse (few nodes, long edges = "highways"). Layer 0 is dense (all nodes, short edges = "local roads").
hnsw.step3: Search starts at the top: from a fixed entry point, greedily walk to the neighbor closest to the query, repeat until no improvement.
hnsw.step4: Drop down a layer and repeat: the position from the previous layer becomes the start of the next layer's walk. Each drop refines the result with finer-grained edges.
hnsw.step5: Layer 0 with ef: at the bottom layer, instead of stopping at the first local minimum, HNSW maintains a pool of ef best candidates and expands their neighborhoods. Larger ef → more accurate but slower.
hnsw.takeaway: Why it's fast: the top layers act like an index over the index — you cover huge distances in just a few hops, then zoom in. For N points, search visits roughly O(log N) nodes instead of N.

diskann.title: DiskANN Explorer
diskann.subtitle: DiskANN keeps a tiny PQ-compressed copy of every vector in RAM and the full vectors + graph on SSD. Search walks the graph with cheap RAM compares, then re-ranks the top candidates with a handful of expensive disk reads.
diskann.memoryWallTitle: Why DiskANN exists: the memory wall
diskann.memoryNote: A single commodity SSD holds the disk side easily. The RAM side fits in any laptop. Without the PQ trick, you'd need a server with hundreds of GB of RAM to even load the dataset.
diskann.modelNote: Modeled with PQ ≈ 1 µs, SSD ≈ 100 µs. Real numbers depend on hardware.
diskann.stepPqCompare: RAM: PQ-compare {{count}} neighbor(s) of node {{node}}. Cheap.
diskann.stepMove: Move from node {{from}} → node {{to}} (best PQ candidate).
diskann.stepDiskRead: DISK: fetch full vector for node {{node}} (re-rank). Expensive.
diskann.step1: Build the Vamana graph: a single-layer kNN graph (degree R) optimized for sequential disk layout. No hierarchy — just one big graph.
diskann.step2: Quantize and split storage: every vector gets a tiny PQ code (e.g. 32 bytes). PQ codes live in RAM. Full vectors + graph edges live on SSD.
diskann.step3: Beam-walk with PQ distances: from a fixed entry point (the medoid), keep a candidate pool of size L. Pop the best, expand its neighbors, estimate distances using only the in-RAM PQ codes. No disk I/O during the walk.
diskann.step4: Disk re-rank the finalists: the candidate pool's top entries had their distances approximated by PQ. Read their full vectors from disk and recompute exact distances to fix the ranking.
diskann.step5: Return top-K. Total disk I/O = a small number of random reads, usually fewer than 20.
diskann.whyScales: Why it scales: RAM only holds compressed codes. A 1B-vector dataset with 32-byte PQ needs ~32 GB RAM — fits on a single workstation.
diskann.whySlower: Why it's slower than HNSW: SSD random reads are ~100× slower than RAM. Even with only ~10 reads, query latency is dominated by disk.
diskann.whyBeats: Why it still beats IVF_PQ for recall: the re-rank step recovers quantization errors that pure PQ-based methods can't fix.
diskann.takeaway: The mental model: PQ is the cheap "is this neighborhood promising?" test. Disk reads are the expensive "let me actually verify" check. DiskANN gets most of its work done with the cheap test.

tradeoff.title: Tradeoff Dashboard
tradeoff.subtitle: There is no single "best" index. The right choice depends on your data size, latency budget, recall target, and memory constraints. Pick a dataset scale and hover the bubbles or cards to compare.
tradeoff.bubbleSizeNote: Bubble size shows memory footprint — smaller bubble = more memory used.
tradeoff.tip1: Top-right is best: high speed AND high recall. Indexes drift down-and-right as data grows.
tradeoff.tip2: Big bubble = small memory. A small dot like HNSW at 10M means it eats RAM. PQ-based indexes stay fat (low memory).
tradeoff.tip3: FLAT is the ground truth. Always 100% recall but speed collapses as the data grows. Use it as the reference everyone else loses ground to.
tradeoff.tip4: Switch the scale to see how the same indexes shift. HNSW dominates at 100K but its memory bill becomes scary at 10M.
tradeoff.takeaway: Decision shortcut: small data → FLAT or HNSW; medium data with RAM → HNSW; medium-to-large data with tight RAM → IVF_SQ8 or IVF_PQ; billion-scale → DiskANN. Always benchmark on your real data before committing.
